So in spring boot generally this layered architecture is something which is mostly used right?
And in many big MNCs you will find this layered architecture only.
So what is this layered architecture?
So let's say these are the clients.
They are hitting any API okay.
So this is the first layer which is controller layer.
So this controller layer has all the classes which has annotation like at the rate controller at the
rate rest controller like this.
So these are the classes which host your endpoints right.
Like we have seen previously.
Like like slash payment slash payment slash some endpoint one.
Like these.
All classes only host that API and client hit that API right.
They will interact your component with the these APIs itself.
And controller layer is the first starting point.
Uh which host hosts those APIs.
Second layer is your service layer.
So these classes generally has at the service annotation.
And this is where we put our business logic.
So all your business logic whatever patterns whatever the business specific logic, anywhere anything
which you have to put, you have to put inside the service layer.
Right.
Not in controller, not in repository service layer.
This is the third layer which is called repository layer.
So there generally you will find classes which is annotated with like at the repository annotations.
And its work is to connect with the DB right.
So maybe my SQL may be NoSQL whatever.
So this layer actually interact with the DB, fetch the data and return to the service layer or if it
has to insert repository layer takes the responsibility to insert the data also.
So repository layer generally and should be interacted with DB.
So many times people what we do is we from service layer.
Also we sometimes start doing get calls and insert calls into DB.
See nobody is stopping it, right?
We can do whatever we want, but it's a not.
You can say that the correct way of doing it, as per the layer architecture repository layer should
be the one which should touch DB.
Okay, so apart from this three layers, there are certain uh, other you can say that packages which
we create as spring.
So one very important is this DTO this is not a layer.
This is you can say that the additional packages we create okay.
So one is DTO.
What is dto?
Let me explain you.
Okay, so see DTO is generally you can say that data transfer object.
Okay.
So now let's say that client is hitting certain post API and they are sending certain body the data
in the body.
Let's say certain ID 123 certain number uh number card or card number.
Let's say certain my card number 54321.
So certain information coming it from the post API.
The client which is sending.
So in the DTO data transfer object.
So generally there are two type we can create.
One is request DTO one is response DTO.
So in the request DTO, what we do is we map App.
The data, which is coming from the client or outside our component to this DTO request DTO so that
our business layer repository whatever gonna work with gonna work with the request DTO not with the
fields which are coming from the external API, like the clients which you are initiating.
Why?
Because let's say that in future the schema got changed and let's say its ID name got changed to certain,
uh, certain this thing like identification, uh, from ID name change to identification or let's say
sequence number.
Okay.
So we have to change at all the places where this ID is used.
So it has to be changed to sequence number.
But if you are using request DTO then at the controller layer itself.
So controller layer takes the responsibility of doing the mapping.
ping from, uh, the parameters from API parameter to request DTO.
And now everywhere everyone works on the request DTO.
So only this controller class will get impacted if any of the schema got changed.
Because this is the place where we are doing the mapping.
Okay.
Similarly, the response DTO.
So now let's say that service layer calls the response repository layer.
Hey fetch me the data repository layer, get the data and return it.
Is it a good way that in the DB, let's say if we have a column name as uh card certain card ID, let's
say I'm just taking that card ID so is it a good way that we return the card ID in the response itself,
our DB column name?
No.
So what we do is the service layer do the mapping, whatever the values which are coming from repository
of a DB, map it to the response DTO what ultimately goes out of your system?

At the rate controller and at the rate rest controllers tell to the spring.

body annotation here.

I have used at the rate response body, but in the rest controller here.
If you see in every method I haven't used at the rate response body.
So what does at the rate response body annotations mean?
So at the rate response body tells that the return type of the method, the return type of the method
should be considered as your HTTP response.
Right?
So if you don't write this, let's say what would happen if you don't write at the rate response body?
What spring boot will do whatever the return type.
So return type is a string.
So let's say I am instead of returning this big I am returning.
Let's say just hello I am returning just hello.
So what Spring Boot will do is Spring boot will try to resolve this.
So it will consider as the name of a view and it will try to resolve this.
Let's say hello.
So it will try to say that.
Hey hello dot JSP.
Is there any view like this.
It will try to look for it and then if it finds it it will try to render it on the UI.
Okay.

which you have to render.
This is a HTTP response Body, right?
Sand.
Sand as it is.
Don't consider it as a view.
Consider it as a rest response.

Each method.
Why?
Because here if you see that in the rest controller, rest controller is nothing but a controller with
response body, right?
So you don't specifically have to mention at every method that the response type should.
Spring boot should not consider the response type as a view name.
Consider this as a rest http response only.
So that's why generally you can say that everywhere you will find we are using Rest controller rather
than controller, because it's pretty convenient that you don't have to put rest response body in each
method.

beans get created.

Not at the time of application startup, but whenever it is actually needed, only that time it is created.

Injected here.

to, for example, in the order class.

So this is the first issue that any change in the structure of order your user class will also get impact.
Like you see now let's say you have done this.

It will break the dependency inversion rule of the solid principle.
So what D says in the solid principle, the D says in the solid principle that do not depend on concrete
implementation, rather depend on abstraction.
So when you are doing this new online order, you are creating an object of an concrete implementation.
You are actually breaking the dependency inversion rule of solid principle.
So to fix it, what you have to generally do is you have to depend on its abstraction interface class.
In this case, right.
And its dependency should come from the external.
So let's say that whoever is going to create an object of user, they will pass, whether online or
offline order that will ultimately get set to here.
So this is what dependency inversion principle says.
So in spring boot this dependency inversion can be achieved through dependency injection.
Okay, so dependency injection solves the above problem.
So what?
Dependency injection says that we can make our class independent of dependencies.

Like spring.

So first thing is all mandatory dependencies are created at the time of initialization itself.

So what are the some of the common issues when dealing with dependency injection?
So two major issue is one is circular and another is your unsatisfied for dependency.
So let's first see the circular dependency.
So in the circular dependency what happens is let's say you have a class order.
You have a class invoice.
Order is dependent on invoice.
Invoice is dependent on order.
So now here if you see that this is a circle circular dependency order is dependent on invoice.
Invoice is dependent on order.
Now they are both using auto wired here.
So when you start an application application will fail to start says that it form a cycle.
So this is a circular dependency.
So how do you resolve it.
The first and the foremost is that we should refactor the code and remove the cycle dependency.
How.
There might be some code here.
Common code which both needed its.
Take out the common code To a separate class.
Right.

And you will find certain new stuff in this that what is profiling okay.

annotation.
Right.
So you already know that what's conditional on property.
If you haven't seen just check it out because they are interconnected.
How?
I will tell you.
So in just one quick recap, at the rate conditional on property, what does it say is that bean is
created conditionally means a bean can be created or not.

So through this annotation here we in the Application.properties Is we provide this configuration SQL
connection dot enabled and this value get match up.
If it is equal then only been will get created.
Otherwise been will not get created.
Okay.
And I have asked this question okay.
If you have two application, if you have two application and one common code base, how you will make
sure that bin is only created for one application, not for other.
Okay, so most of the answers were at the rate conditional on property, which is expected one.
And also many engineers also said that we can also achieve through at the rate profile annotation.
And we will use at the rate profile annotation.
Okay.
So today I will touch up this point because this is very important to understand Stand the proper use
of profile.
So yes, you can achieve the same functionality through at the right profile annotation, but technically
at the rate profile is intended for environment separation rather than application specific bin creation.
What does it mean?
Like don't worry, I will get in depth of this right?
So we will understand the profile better.
But for now you need to see that yes with profile also this is possible to do.
A bin can be created only for one application and for other.
This bin will not be created itself.
But this is not the.
You can say that technically it's not correct, but it can be done.
And we will come to this question again at last, once we understand the at the right profile annotation.
Okay, let's deep dive into the accurate profile annotation to understand it better.
Consider this scenario that you have a DB and you are running certain code in your local machine.
So some some says local machine, some says dev development.
Okay, so username and password which you are using to connect to a database might be certain different.
So it's like dev username dev password.
But if the same code you are running on a stage machine or in a QA environment, right.
So maybe the username and the password might be different to connect to a database okay.
And similarly the same code if you are running in live or production, the username and password might
be different than your local machine.
And then state machine.
Right.
So username and password might be different to connect to a database.
So we have different type of environments when we work the same code.
Okay.
When it refers to a particular configuration.

So in simple term, if you want to explain AOP, we can say that it helps to intercept the method invocation.
Right.
And we can perform some tasks before and after the method.
Okay.
So what AOP says that hey you allow it, allow us to focus on business logic by handling boilerplate
and repetitive code like logging, transaction management, etc..
What I mean to say here is let's say you want to have a certain business logic like this is your current
business logic, but many times there are certain piece of code which we have to write.
Let's say you have to keep logging certain things.
You have to keep logging after your business logic runs and also transaction.
You have to start transaction, you have to end the transaction.
And if some issue comes you have to roll back.
Okay.
So there are few things which is apart from your business logic that is known as boilerplate means which
you have to do.
And these are repetitive means it is not just for one, one class or one method.
If you have 100 methods which required logging, you have to do the same thing.
If you have 100 method which require transactional property, you have to start and end the transaction.
Rollback all these things.
So these are repetitive code in your business repo.
So that's where AOP says that, hey, you focus on business logic.
This boilerplate and repetitive code.
I will take care of it so you don't need to handle.
Put it over here.
You can remove logging and transaction rollback.
All you have is now your business logic.
And somehow this logging transaction management AOP will take care of it.

This repetitive and boiler code like logging and all transaction commands and all these are repetitive
and boiler code.
So all this logic can go to a particular place that we call generally an aspect.

It helps in achieving the reusability and maintainability of the code.
Definitely.

So one thing before we start we have to understand about critical section.
So it's a very simple critical section definition.
So it's a code segment where shared resources are being accessed and modified okay.
So now let's say that this is my shared resource.
You so a cab okay.
So in DB let's say that this car has ID 1001 and currently status is available.
Now there is a code segment where we are accessing this shared resource and also trying to update it.
Okay.
So that's why it's known as critical section.
So now what happens is when multiple requests try to access this critical section data inconsistency
inconsistency can happen.
Now let's say that four people are trying to book a cab parallel.
So now let's say that four requests comes here in the critical section in parallel.
So all will read car with 1001.
Yes, they will all read successfully if status is available.
Yes.
Current status is available.
Updated to book for all four.
They will write.
Your car is booked.
If critical section is not handled properly right.
Then during multiple requests data inconsistency can happen.
So what is the solution?
The solution is usage of transaction.
The transaction helps to achieve asset property we like during first year or second year.
We all have studied asset property in the DB right.
So we just to remind you that the first A in the asset is atomicity.
Atomicity says that in a transaction, if any operation fails, the entire transaction will get rollback.
What does it mean quickly?
Let's say that in a DB A has ₹10, B has ₹20.
Now this is your transaction in transaction.
Let's say you want to debit a ₹5.
You want to credit B ₹5.
This is a particular, let's say transaction one.
Okay.
So now what?
Atomicity says that first these are operation.
Operation one.
Operation two.
Okay.
So let's say the first operation executed, you debited a.
So now it would be like ₹5.
Now during credit some failure happened and this operation got failed.
The credit operation got failed.
So as per the atomicity, if any operation fails, the entire transaction will get rollback.
So entire transaction, whatever the operations are there previous operation will get rollback.
So means this debit operation which got success will also get rollback.
So this instead of ₹5 it will again goes back to ₹10.
Okay.
So that's what actually.
Lead to consistency which says that before and after the transaction our DB should be consistent only.
So my DB was inconsistent.
State ₹10 and ₹20.
Right?
A and B after running the transaction.
Right.
Whether it's a successful or failure, doesn't matter, right?
If it is a success, my DB state would be a would be, let's say ₹5 and B would be ₹25.
So this state of a DB is also consistent if there is a failure.
So again my DB state would be consistent.
Okay.
So this property consistency says that no matter after and before the transaction my DB should be consistent
only there should not be any inconsistency like okay the debit got successfully happen ₹5 but credit
doesn't happen.
So credit is still ₹20.
That is inconsistent state okay.
So both are linked to each other.
Then there is a something called isolation.
Isolation says that even if multiple transactions are running in parallel, they do not interfere with
each other.
So this is just one operation, right?
You need to debit a ₹5 credit B ₹5.
This is just a one transaction.
There could be, let's say multiple transaction running in parallel.
There could be another transaction like debit A ₹2, credit B ₹2.
There could be another transaction credit a debit b something.
Something x y something.
So what I'm trying to say is that multiple transaction can run at the same time, but they will feel
like they are running individually, independently, right?
In an isolation environment, like nobody is interfering to each other.
Okay.
So now let's say that everybody wants to update a transaction one, transaction two, transaction three.
And if there are any more, all wants to update a so transaction makes sure that one only one of them
will take up lock on this and one of them only will update it.
Okay.
And once the lock is free then only it will be given to another transaction and then it will work.
So internally transaction make a proper sequence.
Even though from the outside world we all see that all transactions are running in parallel.
But it's not right internally.
Transactional.
Make use of locking and make sure that the operations are in proper sequence manner.
So each transaction feels that they are running in isolation and no no multiple transaction interfere
with each other.
And the last one is durability, which says that once your transaction, let's say this transaction
done its operation and it done commit.
Now once the commit is happened.
Right.
This data is persisted means now, even if your system crash, this data should not get lost.
Okay, so that's what durability says.
That and all this atomicity, consistency, isolation and durability guarantee is provided by transaction.

applications mostly use uh which DB which have which provide this asset property.

So now here in the hierarchy of transaction manager this is the first the top layer interface.
That is transaction manager Okay.
So this is generally an empty interface.
There is nothing inside it.
So this is the second which is very important interface which we have is platform transaction manager.
Right.
This is again an interface which has this three methods get transaction commit and rollback.
Right now if you just go a little bit up right.
And if you see the interceptor what interceptor does is first create transaction internally.

this three methods you have to implement it okay.

The third part.

Right.
So it provide the default implementation of this.
All these three methods.
Right.
So mostly uh there are different managers right.
Data source manager hibernate JPA, JTA right.
And JDBC.

So this parent class, this abstract class parent class has provided some default implementation.
And this concrete classes anything specific they have to override it.
They are overriding it in their respective classes right.
So this is the third class abstract platform transaction manager which is an abstract class okay.
Which is a child of platform transaction manager.
And it provide an implementation for all these three methods.
Now at the fourth layer, what we have is different type of transaction managers.
As I already told you, we can have many right JDBC you know right.

You have to manually write the query.
Similarly JPA Java persistence API.
So with this one, what you can have is you can create entity which can map with the table.

And most of the Crud operation, you don't even have to write a query itself.

distributed transaction.

--- @Transactional Annotation - Part3 | Isolation Level and its different Types ---

Hey guys.
Welcome to concept Encoding.
This is Shreyansh.
And today in the third part of Transaction Management of Spring Boot we are going to cover the isolation
level.
And this is very very important topic as part of Hld.
Also how do you maintain a concurrency and as well in day to day life if you are working?
So let's start without wasting any time.
So if I want to define isolation level in one line, I would say that it tells how the changes made
by one transaction are visible to other transaction running in parallel.
Right?
If you have confusion with this line, don't worry, we are going to see a lot of examples which will
make clear this sentence.
So if you have to use this isolation, how we can use is that when you are using this annotation at
the rate transaction, you can use this like isolation equals to isolation.
Dot read committed.
There are four types of isolation.
Read.
Uncommitted.
Read.
Committed.
Repeatable.
Committed and serializable.
So there are four types of isolation level.
So which type of isolation you have to use.
You can provide it here.
Let's say if you are not providing anything here, if you are not providing anything here, what would
be the default isolation level.
So C default isolation level depend upon the DB which we are using.
We can't say that hey default is always read committed or repeatable committed.
No it depends upon your DB and it might change in future also.
Okay so let's say all mostly relational database use read committed.
But it is possible that some of the relational database might use repetitive computing, right?
So we have to properly read what our DB are using.
If you are using Postgres or MySQL, you have to read properly that which isolation is the default isolation
IT support, right.
Why it is important.
Too much because of concurrency okay.
If you are using read uncommitted, it has very high concurrency means so many requests can run in parallel.
If you are using serializable, the concurrency is very low.
Very low means very less transaction can run in parallel.
Now you can ask me that concurrency high is good, right?
We want as much transaction can run in parallel.
So we should always use read uncommitted.
No.
So see it's always like if there is an advantage, there is a disadvantage also.
So we have to understand properly in which and when we have to use what.
That is actually what important is.
So before I come to read uncommitted, read committed, repetitive, committed or serious serializable.
Before I come to isolation level, let's first understand these problems.
There are three problems which actually this isolation level resolved.
Okay.
So let's first start with what is dirty read problem.
So what is dirty read problem.
Dirty read problem says that transaction A read the uncommitted data of other transaction.
Okay.
And if other transaction get rolled back, the uncommitted data which is read by transaction A is known
as dirty Read.
Now what?
Let's see this definition with an example.
So let's say this is the time.
This is transaction A.
This is transaction B and this is let's say a current DB status at time T1.
Both the transaction started in parallel begin transaction.
Begin transaction.
And this is the current state of the DB id one two, three.
And status of the column status is currently free at time t2 transaction B is updating the row which
has id one two, three and changing it to booked.
So DB status is currently 123 and status is booked.
But at this point of time transaction B has not committed.
Right.
It is still in progress.
Transaction is still in progress.
It is not yet committed or rollback.
It is still running okay.
So this status booked is This change by transition B, but it is not committed.
This transaction is not committed.
So at time T3 the transaction a read the row with ID 123.
So in the dirty read the transactions.
Also read the uncommitted data.
So even this status which is changed by transaction B is not committed yet.
Still it reads it.
So transaction A is able to read what is the status book.
Okay.
Now at time T4.
Now let's say transaction B some issue has come and does the rollback.
And now you know when rollback happened all the changes which it has done they again goes back to the
previous state.
So it again goes back from to free.
But the problem here is transaction.
Hey has read the Uncommitted Changes book, which is now not there into the DB.
This is known as dirty read transaction.
A is now suffered from a dirty read problem because it has read a row which has uncommitted data.
Right.
So the transaction which is not committed all the changes is also read by other transaction.
Then it is known as dirty read problem.
Okay.
So if this problem is clear let's move to another problem which is called Non-repeatable read problem.
Okay.
If suppose transaction A reads the same row several times and there is a chance that it get different
value than it is known as Non-repeatable read problem.
Let's understand with an example of what this definition says.
Let's say this is the transaction A, this is the current DB status at time T1 transaction started,
and this is the current state of db id one status free at time t2.
A read call has been made by transaction A for the id one.
So what is the status is read free okay.
Now let's say at time T3, some other transaction has happened and changed the status from free to booked
and also committed the changes so committed also.
So at time T4, if transaction A again runs the same query, read row id one.
Trying to read the same row.
It will not get the same data.
It will get data got changed.
Now what is the status getting booked.
Okay so that's what it is called as non repeatable read.
Read the same row several times and there is a chance that you get different value.
So this problem is clear like okay what this problem means that if you read a row multiple times there
is a chance that whatever the value you were getting before, now it's get changed.
And how like this is one kind of a use case where this value get changed.
And the third problem is called the read problem.
The phantom read problem says that if suppose transaction A executes same query several times, but
there is a chance that the rows returns are different itself, then it is known as phantom read problem.
Let's understand this definition with an example.
Let's say at time t one, the transaction a started the transaction begin transaction and this is the
current state of the DB.
Current status is Id1 is status is free ID row ID three is status is booked.
Currently let's say two rows are there.
So at time t two transaction A is trying to read a row where ID greater than zero and id less than five.
So what all rows will come, ID greater than zero and ID less than five.
So this both the rows will come right.
So it will read two rows with ID one and three.
Now let's say at time T3 some other transaction inserted the row with id two and committed also.
So now let's say that this new this is the new row has been added by some other transaction at time
T3 and also committed.
Okay now at time T for the transaction, A is running the same query again.
Okay, read row with id greater than zero and id less than five.
Now this time there is a chance that rows returns are different.
Now three rows are coming ID 123.
Because all three are satisfying this condition ID greater than zero and id less than five.
So this is known as phantom read problem when you are getting different rows itself with the same query.
Let me also tell you about de-blocking because this is also required.
So there are two types of de-blocking one is shared lock, shared lock and another is exclusive lock.
So shared locks is also represented by s exclusive lock is also represented by sometime X okay.
So shared lock is also known as Reed lock, and Exclusive Lock is also known as Right Lock.
Now.
Now let's say that I have a DB in which I have one row called.
Let's say ID is one and status is free.
Let's say one row is present.
So what is read lock or shared lock.
So now let's say that there are two transaction transaction one and transaction two.
So let's say that if transaction one wants to read this id one it can take shared lock.
Right?
I can represent the shared lock with s take on ID one.
It has taken a shared lock on ID one.
So currently somebody is taking a shared lock on this one.
Who?
Transaction one.
Now let's say that transaction T2T2 also want to read this row.
Will it be able to read it?
Yes, because shared lock can be, as the name says, that can be shared by multiple transaction, but
only for the reading purpose.
So transaction two also want to read it.
It can also put a shared lock and it can read this same row.
So what shared lock says that more than one transaction can put a shared lock and you can read the value.
That's all.
You can only read, but you cannot modify anything.
You can only read in other way.
The exclusive lock.

So one of the interview question is that what are the conditions for async annotations to work properly.
So there are two conditions which I would say that.
One is that the async annotation where we are using on top of a method, right.
That method should be in a different class from where it is being called.
Right.
So that is one condition.
And second condition is the method on which this async annotation is used.
It should be public method.
Now the question is why.
So now first let's see this example here.
In this we have a class user controller right.
And this is one method Getuser method when this get invoked, it calls an async method test.
Right.
So and one thing you will notice that this async method is in the same class in which this caller is
present.
And expectation is that this async method will get run in a new thread.
That is my expectation because I have used async annotation on top of it.
But will this work as expected?
No.
Why?
Because these are the two conditions which should get fulfilled that this method on which you have used
async annotation should be in a different class from where it is getting called, and it should be a
public method.

same class as of this caller, right from where it is getting called.

Protected method it only work on public methods.

right?

Interception is not even in the picture.

conditions are met.

--- Spring boot: Filters Vs Interceptors ---

Hey guys.
Welcome to concept encoding.
This is Shreyansh.
So yesterday I made a video on Springboot interceptors.
And I see a lot of comments regarding.
Filters versus interceptors.
And what is filters specifically.
Uh, so I what I anticipated from this is that there are a lot of doubts between when to use filter,
when to use interceptor.
What is the exact difference.
So today I will discuss, uh, about this.
And I hope that after this video your doubts will get clarified.
And like you can easily decide when to go for filter.
When to go for interceptor.
Okay, let's start without wasting any time.
So let's before even seeing further.
Right.
Let's see this one liner definition.
Right.
And even though if you don't understand any of these terms, it's okay.
Because once we go further after that we will come back again.
And then we read this.
It will make more sense.
So filter it.
Intercept the HTTP request and response before they reach to the servlet.
Okay.
And what is interceptor says?
It's specific to spring framework and intercept HTTP request and response before they reach to the controller.
Or I would say that before they reach to the controller and after servlet.
Okay, so read it one more time.
It filter intercept the HTTP request and response before the request.
Reach to the servlet and interceptor is after servlet but before controller.
Okay so just keep this in mind and we'll proceed further.

So we have five categories 1X2XX, 3X4X and 5XX.
So 1XX is very less frequently used.
To be honest.
It is mostly informational.

Then server use this response code and it is also called as.

So server when tells to the client some information this is not the final response.
This is not the final response.
Server might still be processing and then later it might return a final response.
So this one is just an informational one.
And it could be an interim interim response code, not the final one.
Second one is success, which is like whenever the request is success Then we use two x return code.
There are so many return codes.
I will tell you three x axis for redirection.
When you want client to look at some other API or some other resource to get the data.
For X is like a validation error, like something is not either client did not send the correct correct
data to you, or there is some business logic failure and but this is an expected expected failure.
This request should fail.
Okay.
So that's why it's like a validation failure.
This is this five x is server error which is like all this request should get success.
But because of some issue at the server null pointer exception typecast exception something it got failed
which should not be.
And we should always make sure that our component should have five errors as low as possible, so I
will cover the one axis at last.

What is the purpose of this 304 not modified.
So client make a get call server return it with the last modified time in header.
So this is the first step.
Server makes a simple get call some get call server return the data, but along with the data, also
send the last modified time when this data, which I am returning when it was last modified.
Now client cache the response whatever the data response it got for the get call it, cache it now client
make another get call.
Pass this last modified time in header and the header name is if modified since okay.
So client add this data into the header if modified since Sense.
So what we can do is when we are writing a API, right?
And we want to add this functionality, we can read this header if modified, since if it is present
into the get call, what it will do is server will check the particular resource last update time with
what client provided.
Okay, in my db for this row.
User one.
It was last updated on let's say 13th September and if modified since earlier which client earlier when
it make a get call is let's say also is 13th September.
Now let's say that if if there are no change, like the client has the latest data itself, there is
no modification in the DB.
After that, the server will say that 304 not modified server do not load all the data and return all
the data server will simply say that 304 not modified, and client will use its cache data.
But let's say that client data has 12th September, but in DB it's now updated and it has 13th September.
Then what it will do is server will load it, server will process the request as usual and return the
new values.
So that is what the main purpose of 304 not modified.

the patch calls.

Then we have something called 4 to 9.
Too many requests.
So here what happened is that we are putting a rule for example, one user can max make ten calls in
a minute.
This is the maximum calls it can make in a minute.

to 9 too many requests.

Is that like uh, you have one request, one request, which is already in, in progress.
It's not yet completed in progress.

Any API let's say patch API okay.
So mostly 409 conflict is popular with patch, delete and post not with the get.

user.
Slash 123.

So what generally we do is we put a lock, right, let's say in some cache or itself say that, hey,
one, two, three.
Currently will says that some it is locked.
Okay.
So till this request is not completed, this lock would be there.

return 409.

released because we know that 10s is sufficient for a one API to get completed.

responsibility.

in state managed.

the entity manager itself.

are few things which we have to see.
Like what is cascade type?
Now we have seen that when we are providing a mapping like at the rate 1 to 1 cascade equals to cascade
type dot all what it is man like why we are using this.
What's the purpose of it.
So let's see that what all values it can take.
First cascade type.
So cascade type can have six values all persist merge remove refresh and detach.
So without cascade type any operation on parent do not affect child entity.
Managing child entities explicitly can be error prone.
So consider this.
You have a parent entity.
And it has one child entity.
I can say that the life of a child here depends on the parent.
Or you can say that existence of a child entity depends on a parent entity.
If parent entity is destroyed, child entity should also get destroyed.
Okay.
If parent entity is created, child entity is also be created if parent entity updated if required.
If there is any update child entity should also get updated.
Okay, so we can say that whenever a use case where child entity exists and depend upon the parent entity,
this casket type can help us with that in managing it if they are not like if they are independent,
like if you are deleting parent but child entity is not deleted, say it could be an error prone right?
When we are saying one time that child entity existing is depend on parent only for example school and
rooms.
So here room existing is on school.
If school destroyed, room is also destroyed.
Right?
So there is no way where like school is finished.
Then you have still have school rooms available.
So just a use case.
So cascade help in managing that any operation on parent.

But what about get?

So during Get means reading the entity, so does child entities always get loaded with parent entity?
Because if you are trying to fetch parent entity like user details does, user address will always get
loaded.
Answer is yes, or we can or we cannot.
It depends what our use case.
So there are two type of loading.
Eager loading and lazy loading.
So eager loading means associated entity like child entity is loaded immediately along with the parent.
Whenever you do a get call on the parent child entity is also loaded immediately.
And if you see for 1 to 1 and many to one, this is the default mapping.
So here if you see 1 to 1 annotation what is the fetch default type is eager.
Similarly for many to one, if you see it's a functional interface, what is the fetch type?
Default is eager.
Similarly for lazy loading means associated entities not loaded immediately, Only loaded when explicitly
accessed, like when we call.
Let's say for example, I am calling user detail get user address.
So here I am trying to access it.
So it make another Select query.
Okay.
So here if you see it is default for one to many and many to many.
Like for if you see one to many, what is the default fetch type?
Lazy.
Similarly for many to many, what is the default fetch type is again lazy.
So in short, if you are using eager loading and you are querying the parent entity, generally whatever
the select query it will make select, it will make with left join.
Okay, you are like select your parent entity left join your child entity always.
But if you are using lazy loading.
So first it will do only select query on parent entity.
Okay.
And now on demand.
If your code internally doing trying to access the child entity here user address my child right.
If your parent is trying to access the child then it make another Select query with left join okay with
parent and your child.
Then it make another left another select query with left join.
It fetch the data and then return okay.
And another things comes to mind that hey why for 1 to 1 it's uh fetch type is eager why for many to
one fetch type is eager and why many to many and one to many.
It's lazy.
So see, this is my understanding.
Whenever like 1 to 1, many to one means this parent have only one child.
only one child.
So JPA do an assumption that since there is only one child entity, so possibly it might be required
while accessing parent.
And since it's a one, so it might not be a much impact on the performance.
But when we have like one to many, many to many means many child's, how many we don't know do we do
do parent required all the child's okay.
So here JPA don't know that.
So that's why it uses default lazy based on the demand.

1 to 1 bidirectional.
So till now, what we have seen is 1 to 1 unidirectional means you have one parent entity.
It has a foreign key which is a child entity.
And this foreign key on this ID primary key on this table primary key.
And you can only access in one direction parent entity to child entity and there is no way to backtrack.
So in 1 to 1 bidirectional, you can also travel backward from child to parent.
But remember table structure do not even change.
Both entities hold reference to each other.
Means user detail has reference to user address.
User address also has a reference to user details.
User address also has reference to user details, but that doesn't mean that this is also also has a
foreign key of this.
No.
In the bidirectional the this the child right?
The child also has a reference to your parent, but only in object, not in db.
So DB structure would still be the same.
Only one foreign key into the parent entity.
Okay.
But in the object we also hold a reference from child to parent.
I will show you how.
So in the bidirectional there is a concept called owner side.
And the inverse side.
Owner side is generally known as your.
Parent side which holds the foreign key.
Inverse size is generally known as your child entity okay.
So owner side holds the foreign key relationship in a table.
If you see our previous example like similarly I have created a user detailed entity table.

user address entity okay.

Cascade delete and orphan removal C.
Both are different concept in Cascade delete means you are so user has two childs order one order two.
In cascade delete means you are deleting the parent and cascade.
It will delete the child.
Also that what cascade delete is okay if you deleting the parent child will also get deleted.
Orphan removal is you are removing a child.
You are removing the child from the collection, not from the DB.
You are not deleting the child from the DB you're removing from the collection only.

context clear.

Okay.
So now let's start with the specification API.
So in the criteria API there are two major issues.
One is code duplicity okay.
And another is there are lot of code boilerplate.
Let me explain.
You like code duplicity how it is.
So if you see this your criteria API which we are writing, we are writing here.
Predicate.
Right.
We are creating a predicate.
It is nothing but a condition in the Where clause.
It is possible that same condition can be present at multiple methods multiple classes.
So the same predicate is reused at multiple, not reused like duplicated at multiple places okay.
So that is one of the issue with the criteria API there.
Like there are lot of duplicate code can be present in the form of this predicates.
The same conditions can be present at multiple methods.
Okay.
And currently I have only one predicate.
But let's say that this method itself, let's say I have five predicate.

So there are a lot of code duplicate.
So the first challenge which resolves by specification API is code duplicity.
The second challenge is which I told code boilerplate.
If you again see your code of the criteria API, you will see so many like criteria.
You have to create criteria builder.
You have to create criteria query.
You have to add it into from clause.
You have to add it into the Where clause, you have added into the like typed query pagination and then

So form login authentication.
It's a stateful authentication method.
Stateful means your server is maintaining a state of a client okay.
Server maintains a user authentication state AKA also known as session.
So server maintains the session so that user don't have to provide username password every time with
each request.
So in this what we have appended, you have a client or let's say that user and you have a server.
So first time only it provide username and password.
At this point of time server create a session and stores it.
Okay.
Now also send this session back.
Hey, this is your session now.
For all the subsequent requests, the client do not provide username and password.
It just provide the session.
It just provide the session and server validate the session.
If it is validate the request has been processed successfully.
If the session is not valid, whatever the session ID is provided by the client.
If it is not valid or not found, the request is not entertained.
Okay, so that's what it means.
So that user do not have to provide username password with each request for.
With each request, they provide a session so user enters their credential in an HTML login form.
This is for the first time on successful authentication.
A session is created to maintain the user authentication state across different requests.
Okay, so server is the one who creates a session and stores it.
Okay.
And also in the response it send.
Now with subsequent request, client passes this session ID not username and password.
Very important it do not pass username and do not pass username and password.
It only passes the session ID and server validates it with the session ID which is stored internally.

So there are few questions can come sometimes during discussion how to control the session per user.
Now let's say that I have one user.
User one.
It is using browser one and it has created session here.
Now the same user using browser two or another window created another session incognito mode or another
session two same user session three.
What if it creates 1000 of session?
So we have to control the sessions right per user.
So we can also control it very simple way.
There is something called session management.
In the session management for session we can set the maximum session is one and after that max sessions
prevent login.
True.
So with this two field set like one one time, you are able to do create a session works fine.
Second time when you are trying to do it will say that maximum session of one user for this principle
exceeded.
Okay, so it will try to like with the username.
It will try to match okay, because with username only it fetches the data from the DB.
So only one user per session is allowed.
After that, if it exceed it will throw an exception.
If you want multiple session for you can just increase this 234 something like that.
There are also some time ask this what is session creation policy?
We haven't discussed anything like that right.
But if you see that dot session management, dot session creation policy is required.
Even if you don't write this, this is by default added if required.
So what is this session.
Creation policies.
So there are four values it can take if required.
Always.
Never.
Stateless.
If required means HTTP session is only created when needed.
And this is the default.
That's why above we haven't used it.
What it mean if only required.
So let's say that I have a public API.
I have a public API.
Okay, for that one HTTP session will not be created okay.
So it only created for which it is required.
Wherever authentication is required it creates HTTP session for that only.
But if you change it and use always always then no matter what HTTP session is always created.
If already HTTP session is present then it will reuse it.
But no matter whether it's a public API or not, a public API HTTP session would get created.
Okay.
But generally for public APIs, authentication is not required.
So generally you don't require you don't need to maintain the state because because let's say public
API is hit by uh, millions of times, you don't have to maintain an authentication.
It's just like informational.
Okay.
So that's why if required is generally preferred.
And it is default.
You don't need to create a session for each APIs.
Also for whichever it is not required.
Never says that.
Do not create a session.
Always use if present.
Okay.
And stateless is like no session is created at all.
It is used for stateless application like JWT.
Okay.
So this is what session creation policy mean.
And this is how generally we set.
And the last question which can come in like what are the disadvantage of form based authentication?
I think by this point of time you already got it.
Like first is like as we have discussed in the previous, vulnerable to security issues like CSRF,
we have already discussed very beginning all the attacks with demo.
Okay, how the CSR demo session hijacking is possible.
So form based authentication is vulnerable to it.
But if you see that by default CSRF is enabled for form based login, you can disable it.
Also in the security filter chain you can say that CSRF disable, but we should not do this for form
based authentication.
Always enable it CSRF right?
Because it is vulnerable to attack like CSRF, so do not disable it.
This is just for sample.
I am showing you.
You can disable it and session hijacking So you know that the session ID is passed in the cookie.
So it is very much likely that your session get hijacked.
It got stolen.
And using this your session ID, the attacker uses it.
Your resources access your resources.
The second is the session management is a big overhead.
So currently everything we have seen is handle to framework.
But I told you that when in 2015 and 16 we were working in a company, it was handled all manually like
because as per the business lot of things in the session, we have to manage according to business need,
right.
So generally if it is a very simple business then yeah, you can use framework.
But definitely as the business grows lot of custom needs required.
So you have to manage the session yourself.
So session management is a big overhead.
You have to have like a session tables, you have to manage its expiry and all this stuff.
Right.
And also now in case of distributed system like it would be like a scalability issue for sure.
And this is the subsequent issue is that database load.
Now you have in the distributed system let's say you have multiple services.
Or let's say that even for one service you have multiple instances.
Definitely you need a DB.
Then like you can't have a session stored in memory.
What if, as I told, because nowadays we have load balancer in front of the client, it can pass the
request to anybody.
So if earlier session is created here, user says that you are authenticated.
Now the second time it tries to access the control goes to the second instance.
Now you say that hey where is the session or and it will say that hey authenticated slash login page
again.
So this is a bad user experience.
Okay.
So that's why in memory is not used.
If you have multiple instances, you have to put into DB so that all the instances fetch the session
from the DB.
So there are multiple server.
Then we need to store in the session into db or cache.
So again memory you require lookup time is required which increases the latency.
Okay I hope this form login authentication is totally clear right?
Do practice it with your hand even though it is not very frequently used.
Nowadays JWT is very popular, but this is the fundamental if our this flow is clear, right?
All other authentication method, we will get to know that how they are working.
Why exactly we need those okay.

Hey guys.

is basic authentication.
So till now we have seen the form login authentication method and which was a stateful authentication
method.
And this one is stateless authentication method.
So stateless authentication means server do not maintain the user authentication state.
No session is maintained by the server.
Okay.
So why it's called Basic as from the name it is clear.
It is basic like very very simple.
In this client has to pass the username and password with every request with every request.
If you are accessing the resources 100 times, you have to pass the username and password with each
and every request, right.
And we have to pass this in the authorization header.
So this is the form in the authorization header.
We have to form pass like this.
It goes like this in your request header.
So first one is basic which tells us it is a basic authentication method we are using.
Then your username colon the password which is base64 encoded.
So remember that it is always in an encoded form not encrypted form.
Right.
So if anybody intercepts your request they can easily decode your username and password.
That's why we call it like always use it on Https.
Never use it over HTTP basic authentication.
Never use it basic authentication over HTTP.
Because in the header we pass username and password and that is only encoded from not encrypted form.

So your username and password goes into the header authorization header.
So this is the authorization field inside an header.
And this is how it goes.

So if you open the basic authentication filter.

the basic authentication filter.
There you can check this the request try to fetch from the header authorization header.
It will try to fetch.
And there it will decode it.
And once it decoded you can see that this was your header.
And after decoding it you can see your username and password.
It is easy very easy to decode.
If it is not encrypted then you can know that if anybody got your, uh encoded form, they can decode
it.
It is nothing but a base64 only.
Okay, so this is how generally it send in the request and you have to pass it in each and every request.
Okay.
So one question comes to our mind that, hey, why we need to send the username and password in authorization
header only.
Why not?
I can't pass it in request body or query parameter or any other way.
Like why only header?
So these are the three possible reasons in my view.
First thing is standard we have to follow one standard.
Let's say if we don't follow any standard, one client can pass you into a request body.
Another client pass you in query parameter.
Another client pass you in header.
So it would be very difficult to deal with different clients.
Okay.
So there is an RFC 7617 HTTP standardization there.
It mentions that all the credentials has to be passed in header only.
And it is important for universally accepted standard across APIs and client so that no matter what
clients what APIs, if you have to pass the credential, you have to pass it into header.
So that's a standard so that you can write frameworks on top of it.
Okay.
The second thing is security.
There are many web servers which log the request body and even the query parameters.
Okay.
And that is not safe because they might be logging it for debugging or analytics purpose.
What if your username and password got logged inside that.
Right.
And you know that sometimes the logging tools which we use in a company, it belongs to a third party.
It's not yours.
Okay.
So this is a security breach.
But generally headers are not logged.
We never log headers.
Right.
So it reduces the risk of exposure.
Okay.
So security is one another big reason why header is chosen that yes we can pass username and password
inside an header.
And the third one is support for all HTTP requests in my view.
If you see that post and put yes, we have a request body and we can pass it.
But what about the get generally get do not have body.
So generally if we have a header then it can support whether the API is get put post whether the API
accept body query parameter or not.
So header would always be there.
So it to maintain a consistency.
Also this required.
So this is my reason like why I think that authorization header is preferred for the username and password.
Okay if that is clear.
Now let's understand little bit more about basic authentication and its flow.

as a JSON object.

So what it does is it will convert this string to AST abstract syntax tree.
So now you will see the tree like this.

And this is the operation end.

Right operand.

So and write its operation end.
So it invokes this method then it's resolved its left operand.
Left operand is your this zero part.
And again if you see this, if you go inside this left operand, again it's a recursive call.
If there is any other operand let's say or so it will goes to that class that will have left right.
Likewise.
And once that is resolved then it will automatically resolve the right operand and the same recursive
operations happen.
And ultimately what it does is once it invokes all the method, all the variables references.

Ultimately what it will do is this interceptor validate the result and decide whether to invoke the
method or not.
So these are the steps involved once the interceptor is invoked.

Why we call it as legacy or traditional way of communication in spring application.
So the one of the biggest problem, which I feel is in the rest template, there are so many overloaded

So this method which I have covered write, execute, exchange delete put post post for object, get

So this is one of the biggest challenge.
Second is this template was built before the concept like retry, circuit breaker etc..
So yes there is a support present into the rest template, but it's more you can know that more overloaded
methods are there.
That's why I tell you that if you want to use a circuit breaker functionality, you will find one overloaded
method for this.
For this, because you have to support for all any new feature you have to add.
Just see that any new feature you have to add, you have to add for all the flavors get post, put,
delete, exchange.
So means like you have to keep on adding the overloaded methods for each of the new functionality you
introduce.
So it's not user friendly to use and it is not easy to remember.
Not easy to maintain.
So that's why Rest template is in maintenance mode.
No new feature, only bug fixes because nowadays in the HTTP request itself, there are so many new
new features coming like this functionality, this functionality like filtering, interceptor functionality.
So means they are not keep on appending new functionalities here now.
That's where the rest client comes into the picture.

Method one.

We got some object.
Which opens up different set of methods.
So I have from method one I reach to method four.
From method four I reach to method six.
From method six I reach to some method eight, something like that.
So there is a chaining of method Okay.

This is class two.
This is class three.
This is class four.

I am doing a method chaining, and this method might be present in two different different methods.

--- Service Discovery and its Implementation ---

Hey guys, and welcome to Concept Encoding.
And today in microservices topic I am going to cover a very important part is service discovery.

And also we will see the implementation also how we can set up the service discovery servers and client.
And we'll see the demo also okay.
So in microservices service or component are created and deleted dynamically.
We can know that right.
So let's say our instance is itself.
Let's say we have our microservices order.
Definitely in microservices one of the advantage is that we can have as many instances based on the
traffic, but these number keep on changing.
If traffic is more, I can spawn more instances.
If traffic is less, I can reduce the number of instances.
So instances are created and deleted dynamically.
We cannot hardcode the URL of a particular instance.
It's not scalable and not feasible.
So till now, while we seen rest template, rest client, feign client order services, making call
to product service and everywhere we have hard coded the URL.
So it's okay for the testing perspective.
But in production we never hard code these URLs, right?
So in above example order service has hard coded the URL of the product service.
And it is okay till we have only one instance of product service.
If you have only one instance then it's okay.
You can hard code it.
But in case if you have multiple product service instance right.
And which is very very possible right.
Let's say in this scenario.
So I have instance one, instance two instance three all have different your IP address and port number
all has different IP address and port number, but if you have hard coded the URL, your traffic will
always go to one of the service only always goes to one of the service only.
Okay.
And it is also possible that a particular stance can be removed, but since you have hardcoded it,
you will try to connect to an instance which is removed.
Then you will face connectivity issue, right?
So there are lot of challenges like first is single point of failure.
Single point of failure is is hard coded instance of product service goes down order service will not
be able to communicate with other instance.
That is what I told.
Like if you have hardcoded it and I have removed this instance, then you won't be able to communicate
it.
So it's a single point of failure.
No load balancing.
So even though I have three services, since you have hardcoded it every time the traffic goes to this
instance only.
Even though my these two other instances are sitting idle.
Okay.
Tight coupling.
So because of hard coded URL in the order service, there is a tight coupling between order service
and product service.
Now let's say that I have to move the product service okay.
And maybe its IP and port address need to be changed.
Then you first have to update the order service.
And then only you can migrate your product service.
Otherwise it's not possible.
Okay.
So it brings the tight coupling difficulty in testing different environment might use different URL
which require frequent changes in your config.
So if you have hard coded it let's say now you are testing on r dev.
Then you need to change this.
If you're testing on stage you need to change this okay.
So with different test environment uh, production QA dev different URL.
And then you have to keep on changing it if you are doing a hard coded.
So one of the way we can overcome all this limitation is our service discovery.
And there are, uh, different, uh, tools like Eureka or Console.

So but you can also go with the console also, the concept wise, very much similarity.

So first thing is how does Eureka server knows whether a client is up or not.
So Eureka server know by two way.
First is client de registration request.
Another is through client heartbeat.
Let's say that first when client application is gracefully shut down, then Eureka client sends a De-registration
request to Eureka Server.
It marked the client status as down.
Okay, so now let's say that currently product service is up.
Okay, now I shut down this product service application from the ID.
I shut down this application.
So if you see the logs you will see that hey it will call Unregistering application with Eureka with
status down it is calling the Eureka server with status down.
And after that and if you check the server Eureka server logs it, received it registered instance this
with status down and then it updated the status with down for this one.
Okay, so one is through.
If gracefully things happening, then de-registration call happen from client to server.
And with that Eureka server know that client is down.
But now let's say assume that your client got shut down, but without any without sending any de-registration
request, maybe because of network issue or something, then how it will know.
It will know through heartbeat.
Every client periodically sends heartbeat to the Eureka server.
Okay, so Eureka server, wait for the heartbeat from the client for a particular interval.
We can we can configure this interval.
I will show you okay.
So Eureka server wait for the heartbeat from client for a particular interval.
If no heartbeat received within that time interval it remove the client instance itself.
It consider it dead.
So consider this client application dot properties okay.
Let's say product service.
So this things we have set the port number application name, the Eureka server URL and registry discovery.
We have already set this.
Now concentrate on this two new config.
The first is eureka dot instance dot lease renewable interval.
So lease renewable interval in second means that after this much seconds client will set the new heartbeat
to the server.
So I said 60 means after 60s the Eureka client will send the heartbeat to the server.
If you don't set it by default is 32nd, then another config is Eureka dot instance dot lease expiration
duration in second lease expiration.
This is like telling server to wait only this much time for the heartbeat.
After that you can remove it from the list.
Default is 92nd.
So this means that client is telling to the server that hey, this is the maximum time you can wait
for my heartbeat.
If you don't get the heartbeat within this time, you can consider me as like unavailable.
So in this example, generally this time is greater than this time.
But for this example only I have taken it very less.
So after 60s the client will send the heartbeat to the server.
But server will wait only for five seconds.
Okay.
So it will mark it as dead.
We will see that.
And in the Eureka server application dot properties, what we have to do is we have seen this config
already.
Now this two config we have to set first is unable Eureka server unable self preservation false.
This by default this is true.
We have to make it false because we need to allow server to remove the instance if heartbeat is not
received.
So if it is generally true right.
So generally even though the heartbeat is not received the server do not remove it.
Okay.
so if you make it false, then means we are allowing the server.
Hey, if a heartbeat is missed, feel free to remove it.
Okay.
And the second is that eviction interval timer.
So sometimes what happens is like even the even the client is down.
Even the client is down.
But the server has to run like server check for the dead instances.
Okay.

--- Client Side Load Balancer and its Implementation ---

And today in microservice topic we are going to cover Load Balancer.
So let's start without wasting any time.
So we all know what load balancer is.
Generally it helps in distributing the traffic to multiple instances of a server.
Right.
Which helps in preventing single server from being overloaded with huge traffic.
This is very, very basic definition of load balancer, which we all know, but things got more interesting
when we go into its type.
So generally it's of two type server side load balancer and client side load balancer.
So in server side load balancer what generally happen is it has centralized load balancer right.
So here if you see this is your client let's say this is our Spring Boot app.
This is our Spring Boot app which we have written.
Now we are calling a dedicated you can say that a server which do the role of load balancing.
So it has a centralized load balancer.
Okay.
So our client do not contain any logic of load balancing.
Okay.
So it calls a dedicated load balancer server which does and forward the request to one of the service
requested service.
But in the client side load balancer what generally happens is load balancing capability present inside
the client.
The caller itself okay.

if you remember.

Welcome back to the channel.
And today in microservices topic we are going to do how to build a fault tolerant microservice right.
So let's start without wasting any time.
So when anybody says that what is fault tolerant microservice, we mean to say that a service which
continues to work even when downstream system fails.
Okay.
So instead of crashing or cascading failure, it should handle the failure gracefully.
So what?
In short, you have a system build up with multiple microservices, even if some of the microservices
are down and your microservices are depend on them, you should not crash.
You should handle the failure gracefully.
Okay, then we can say that it's a fault tolerant microservice.
Okay.
Why it is required.
You will get more clarity with this, uh, example like why it is required.
So see, as in microservices, different services communicate over the network.
We all know this.
And it's possible that one service unavailability could bring down the whole system.
See this example.
Consider that this system is not fault tolerant.
How a one service can bring down the whole system.
In a normal scenario, let's say there could be multiple microservices, which I'm calling as a client,
right?
So let's say some other client or microservices is calling order component order is calling product
products.
Give the response and order in return gives the response to the client.
This is a normal flow.
Now let's say a buggy code has been introduced in the product and the product service becomes too slow.
This product becomes too slow.
Each API is now taking let's say 60s, right?
60s is too much in distributed system, right?
Say product service become too slow and taking 60s to respond.
If proper fault tolerant is not applied, then a call from order service will hang for 60s.
Now the order service which is calling the product service right need has to wait for 60s.
What if there is a sudden burst of requests coming from client?
Then all the requests will wait for 60s, right?
Just see this example.
Now a sudden burst coming from a microservice to a order component order in return.
Calling the product and product is currently going too slow.
So all the threads are now waiting.
Okay, all the threads are now waiting.
Now what eventually will happen is service will run out of thread and start rejecting the request order.
You know that there is a concept of thread pool.
We have already covered that in depth.
So ultimately when all the threads are waiting, the thread will ultimately will get finished, all
threads will get finished, and there you will reach a point when you will start declining the request.
And this becomes a cascade failure to all your client and microservices also.
And also one more thing is even the product component will not get a normal situation because the.
You can say that the situation of a product service will keep on deteriorating because more and more
load is coming to the product.
So even though the issue is at product, the failure got cascaded to order and may be because of order
has stopped.
Even order can get slow, order component can get slow or if it's thread is all done, it will start
rejecting the request.
This failure can cascade to other components also.
So that is known as cascading failure.
And now you can see that how a one service can bring down the whole component whole system.
So that's why we need fault tolerant.
That's why we need to build a fault tolerant microservice.
So if you are building a order, it need to be fault tolerant that even if product goes down or product
goes slow, I should not like its failure, should not get cascaded to me and I should not get crashed
to.
Okay, so here comes our resilience for framework.
So resilience for J is a combination of mechanism like rate limiter, bulkhead time limiter, circuit
breaker and retry.
So these five are the combinations.
Or you can say that the mechanism which resilience for J provides which helps to develop a microservice
as fault tolerant.
And one more thing.
I would say that we will go one by one in a recommended logical ordering.
So you might say what is the ordering issue like.
We can we can apply anything in in whichever order.
But see this is the logical order rate limiter first, then bulkhead, then time limiter, then circuit
breaker and then retry.
Right.
Why?
Because there is a reason.
Like let's say that if you are applying right, uh, for example rate limiter should always be applied
before retry.
Now let's say you are applying first retry and then rate limiter.
This mechanism you are applying in your system like this.
So does that make sense to you.
Just think what if rate limiter is that it.
Whenever an access traffic comes right it blocks some of the traffic.
Now, if you do the retry mechanism first means you are unnecessarily wasting your computation in retrying
on those traffic that ultimately will get blocked by the rate limiter.
Okay, so there is no point of retrying those traffic which ultimately get blocked.
So that's where rate limiter should be first and retry should be second in this sense.
So that's why if when you are applying all the five mechanism in your system, logical ordering should
be present.
That's what there is no hard and fast rule.
But this is you can say that are de facto, which generally should be followed like this should be the
order.
Okay.
So let's start with our first mechanism which is rate limiter.
Very very important from interview perspective.
Even when you are building the services, also in the product based system like that's also is very
important.
So rate limiter is what it controls.
The number of requests allowed to a microservice in a given time window.
Let's say that in this time window a particular time window limited time window let's say five second
ten second, whatever, how much requests can go through.
So in short, it protect our system from sudden traffic spike like a DDoS attack.
Okay, so there are multiple mechanisms or algorithms in rate limiter before even we goes to the implementation,
let's quickly see what are all the algorithm.
So there are most commonly five algorithm fixed window counter sliding log sliding window counter which
again has two flavors token bucket and leaky bucket.

we will at least get to know what rate limiter actually does internally.

Then we have token bucket.
Token bucket is very, very simplified versions.
And uh what happen is we have a kind assume it like we have a bucket and we place the limited tokens
inside it.
Each request consume one token.
If there is no token left, request is denied.
And there is also a refiller which adds the token in bucket at regular time interval.
If bucket capacity is full and cannot hold more token, token overflow will happen and token will be
rejected.
So understand with this is that this is one bucket which has capacity of four token.
So 12344 tokens are present.
There is a refiller which task is to add one token every six seconds.
Okay.
So now lets say that at 12345 second five requests comes up.
So first four because we have four tokens right.
So first request take one token.
Accepted accepted accepted accepted.
Now all four tokens are used.
Okay.
Now fifth request.
Come.
Any token left.
No.
So it is rejected.
Now at sixth second, a refiller adds one token.
So Refiller added one token into the bucket because we have configured it.
Add one token every six second.
So at six second one token is added.
Now if any request comes, it will use it and accept it.
Very very simple.
So disadvantage is that a quick burst is possible if token capacity is not set properly.
What if I have put a token that in a bucket I have capacity equals to 1000 I.
Without doing proper computation, I give random capacity.
Then means that much token can get accumulated.
And what if in a one second itself sudden request comes all 1000, so all 1000 will get accepted.
So there is a chance of burst traffic, okay.
Because whatever the amount of tokens present, that much concurrent request can be accepted.
So you have to consider this token capacity properly.
Okay.
Another is leaky bucket.
Leaky bucket is considered as like request are queued and accepted at fixed rate.
If queue is full, incoming request will overflow.
So let's say you have an incoming request.
Okay bucket.
Or you can say that a queue which is keep on accumulating the request.
Okay.
And the queues are accepted at a very constant or very fixed rate.
So consider this with the flow diagram a request comes if queue is full.
If this queue is full then it will send denied and it will send http 4 to 9.
Too many requests can't even accept.
If queue has some space then it will add the request at the end of the queue and from the queue.
Our system will keep accept the request in a very constant rate.
Very very constant rate.
So very simple.
But disadvantage is your latency will increase because requests might have to wait in queue for its
turn.
So request which is present here.
It has to wait for the previous request to finish and to be accepted.
And your queue is become a bottleneck.
So site size should be set properly.
If the size of the queue is very small, you would be denying lot many requests.
If the size is too big, your latency will increase a lot.
