--- Retry Pattern ---

Hey guys and welcome back to the channel.
So today we will start with retry.
And we have already covered rate limiter in part one bulkhead and part two time limiter.
We are not going to cover.
We will cover it when we will do reactive programming.
So let's start without wasting any time.
And uh, before I start I just want to say that that retry, if in not properly implemented it has more
disadvantages than advantage.
We will get to know why.
So in distributed system call to downstream service might fail because of transient issues.
Transient issues means that short temporary issue like network issue, timeout, etc. and retry of same
request after a short delay can get succeed.
Okay, so one question might comes to you that hey still why we should do retry?
Why can't user or client can invoke our API again.
So let's say that this is our client.
This is our component order.
And we have an API.
And we are invoking another API of let's say product component.
And there is some transient issue while invoking it.
So what we are saying is so transient issues, if we retry it, it can get succeed.
So we can do a retry for this invocation.
So the question is that hey why we need to retry.
Why can't client again invoke the API so that automatically it will invoke the product API again.
Why we have to retry ourself.
So see the answer is for each call lot of heavy processing is done and it's not free of cost, memory,
time, resources, etc. everything like it's there is some cost incurred.
So say you have invoked client at first invoke an API.
90% of the API implementation is completed.
Task is completed.
Only 10% is left.
And then it invoke this product API and the transient issue happen.
Let's say uh network timeout happen.
So is it a good idea to dispose of this 90% of the task which is done?
No.
Right.
We if we can just simply retry this, and if it gets succeeded, then only 10% task have to be done.
Otherwise, if we ask client to retry again, our API hit again, our API again we will do the same
90% task again.
So redundant of the work is there, duplicate of the work is there and it will increase our cost and
it's not free of cost to be honest, right?
So with above understanding, we know that retry is important, right?
I think I hope this point will tell you that why retry is important?
Because we don't want to do the same task again.
Okay.
And I'm just saying this is just one client.
If there are hundred clients for this API and they are all invoking and there are thousands of calls,
so you are saying that you are doing lot of wasting your, uh, capacity on just duplicate tasks which
you have already completed.
So we know that retry is important.
But now question is when to retry.
How to retry.
Very very important question when to retry.
First of all is do not retry on permanent failures.
Permanent failures is like no matter how many times you will retry, it will always fail.
Like for Forex errors, so forex errors generally are in the category of validation error.
Validation error.
Validation error means the input request input request which client has passed has is not correct until
unless client will not change, the input validation error will not remove.
So if you retry with the same request previously again you will see a validation error.
So generally these are known as permanent failure.
For xx.
Do not retry if operation is non idempotent.
What does it mean.
So hydro patency makes sure that if request is retried multiple times duplicate processing will not
happen.
So say you have a service A which invokes the create API of service B okay.
So it has invoked it.
And while service B writes into its DB, but it takes certain time.
Okay.
And here it says that timeout happen and timeout happens.
So for service A the request has been timeout.
But service B internally inserted the row into the DB.
Now since this is a timeout and user has retried.
So this is a retried again.
Now here service B insert the row again Id2.
Earlier it was already inserted Id1.
Now it is Id2.
So here.
This is not my intention.
My intention was not to create a duplicate row for the same request.
Okay.
So we can say that this this API, the API of the service B, this create API is not impotent okay.
So that's why we say that do not retry if operation is not idempotent.
Otherwise you will end up in creating duplicate resources.
So if you don't know how to design APIs, I have already covered it into the high level design playlist.
Uh, in depth.
Like what are the challenges, how we can come up with the API design?
So have a look in case you have a doubt.
Okay, so but here one thing is we don't have to retry if operation is not repeated.
The third is that rule of thumb says that retry only on five error.
What five error says that it is system error.
Your input request.
Input request is good.
Okay, all validation passed, but there is some limitation in system or some issue happen in the system
because of that error has happened.
So generally these are considered as retrieval error.
Okay.
So it is safe to retry on this because this is first of all system error and also it says that it request
is not successful.
Means the previous request five access means request is not successful.

And we can retry as many times so there is no harm okay.
And five has already know that input request is correct.
No validation errors.
But there is some issue with the system.
So with retry it is possible that it might get resolved.
So that is one.

Now how to retry.
So see this is very important part.
So let's see first types of retry and when.
And then we will see how to implement it also.
So first is fixed interval retry.
So what does fixed interval retry says that retry after a constant delay.
So within each retry the delay is constant.
So say that the attempt total attempt is four.
So when we say total attempt means original one call plus three retries, so total four.
Okay, so after original call there has to be a fixed delay.
Like let's say my fixed delay is two second.
So it would be like after two second first retry will happen.
Then it will wait for two second then second retry will happen.
Then it will wait for two second, then third retry will happen.
So there is a fixed interval of delay within the retries.
So let's say this is the original call which happened at two.
So first retry happen at four.
Then second retry will six third retry at eight.
So each one has two two seconds gap same.
So advantage of this is it is very very simple easy to configure and debug also.
But this is main disadvantage is that higher chance of retry storm which is also known as thundering
herd problem.
Thundering herd problem in, many clients retry at the same time, causing a surge of retry traffic
and system spent resources on retries instead of handling new requests.
So understand with this is that.
Say that you have a load balancer and you have, let's say component service a service a multiple instances
instance one, instance two, instance three, and load balancer distribute the traffic between the
instances of service a there are so many clients client one, client N and the traffic is also huge.
Now let's say that a buggy code has been introduced into the service a and it is.
Start throwing some exception and if client start doing retry also, since already it's a heavy traffic
and for that they are all started doing retry at a fixed delay.
So all are retrying.
So original request.
Let's say there are 1000 request original.
Now after two second delay.
Retry one will happen.
Okay.
So everyone will do retry 1000 requests again after two second delay.
Retry two.
They again do a retry okay.
So these are just retry traffic whatever the normal traffic new request is coming.
That is different.
So means your system is now get bombarded with retry traffic.
And it is possible that while you are only servicing retry traffic, you somehow decline the new request
which is coming.
That is also possible.
Okay, so I if you remember like in the playlist.
I have already covered Thundering Herd problem like it is one of the frequently asked interview question.
So if you want to know more about Thundering Herd problem, you can check it out.
But yeah, this is one of the disadvantage is that if all the clients retry at the same interval, it
is possible that the retry traffic become huge and it can cause thundering herd problem.
So fixed interval is clear.
Then we have exponential backoff.
So what does exponential backoff says.
That delay increases exponentially.
So currently earlier you are delaying at this time.
Then this time then this time not at fixed rate, not after two seconds.
This is two second not at fixed.
So it increases with the time.

--- Circuit Breaker ---

Hey guys, and welcome back to the channel.
And till now, in the fault tolerant microservices we have seen rate limiter bulkhead retry.
And today we will see a circuit breaker.
So let's start without wasting any time.
What does Circuit Breaker says?
This pattern prevents an application to make repeated calls to downstream service that is likely to
fail.
Okay, so we'll come to this line again.
But see this diagram.
So let's say our application is order service right.
And we and this product is a downstream.
So order is invoking product for.
Now assume that this product service is unavailable.
So no matter how many times you call this call will fail.
Right.
So product service is down no matter how many times whether you are doing retry of the same call or
whether you are making a totally different call, it will fail.
So there is some disadvantages for it.
The first disadvantage is we put unnecessarily add load to the product service and because of that product
service might take longer time to recover.
So see product services already unavailable right.
It is facing some issue and we are adding more load to it.
So there is a chance that product service might take little longer time to, uh, come up in the healthy
state.
The second is order services unnecessarily wasting its resources, both latency and the thread blocking.
So just see that if, let's say there are thousands and thousands of calls and product service is unavailable,
they all will fail.
So Order service is unnecessarily wasting its resource because there is a latency involved.
It calls it.
It waits for the product service to response till timeout, and then only it will send to the client
that hey, it's a fail some five secs error something.
Okay.
So there is definitely increase in latency and you are unnecessarily blocking your thread on this call
okay.
So there is no need because even if you wait it, the call is likely going to fail because the product
service is unavailable.
But now what is the solution?
How does Order Service know when to stop the downstream call?
Like we are saying that hey order don't make unnecessarily call to product because it is unavailable,
right?
But how?
Order knows that when it is unavailable, when it is up and when I have okay if it is unavailable,
when again I have to restart.
So something has to instruct this up.
You can say that knowledge somebody has to put this knowledge to order right.
Then only order would be able to stop or restart the call.
So who at that knowledge that is circuit breaker.
Okay.
So again revisiting the line which we went ahead in the first place, it prevents an application to
make repeated calls to downstream service that is likely to fail.
So see this diagram now.
So now order service let's say make initial calls.
And both got failed.
So now we will put some threshold value that hey if this many calls get failed means there is some issue
with the product.
So don't make any further calls to it.
So now after that, once that threshold limit is reached, we will stop the future call to product.
So now all future calls.
There is no hit to the product.
We will fail the call immediately.
We will not block the thread.
We will fail the call immediately.
Okay?
And we will wait for some time.
After that we will check if product service is healthy or not so it will do a trial testing after some
time.
Hey, it will send some sample or you can say that the test calls hey is product service is responding
properly or not.
If yes then only it will allow future calls to the product.
Okay, so whatever we have covered here it is can be understand.
It can be understood through this state diagram.
So start from here.
This is the initial state.
Okay.
So for any application the best case is closed.
Closed means that all calls are allowed to downstream service means everything is going good.
Okay.
Circuit is closed.
Means just remember always sometime, many times engineers get confused that hey, circuit is closed.
Means no calls.
No.
So see, consider this as a circuit.
So when the circuit is closed, then only electricity will flow.
When the circuit is open, then electricity current will break.
Understand?
It is just to remember this.
So when the circuit is closed, all calls are allowed to downstream service.
Now if failure limit cross the threshold.
let's say five failure, six failure, whatever the threshold we will set if that many failures.
So see, there could be some success, there could be some failure while making a call to downstream.
But if the failure limit threshold value is crossed, then it moved from closed state to open state.
Now, open state means no calls allowed to downstream service and fail the call immediately.
So after this it wait for certain time, which is like you can say that timeout period.
After that let say it could be ten second, five second, 15 second, that hey, for five second or
ten second, no call is allowed.
After that it moved to half open state.
Half open state says that allow only limited number of test calls to downstream Stream and track their
success rate.
Say, I said that.
Hey!
Allowed only three calls.
Allowed only five calls.
So very limited number of calls.
We will send it to the downstream and then track their success rate.
If the success rate is 100% then circuit can be closed again.
But if the success rate is not 100%.
The state goes back to open.
Okay, so whatever we have seen this can be easily represented through this state of circuit breaker.
Okay.

So what this feature provides, it provides a single entry point to access all microservice.

--- ApiGateway in Microservices - Part2 - Authentication JWT, Circuit Breaker etc ---

Hey guys, and welcome back to the channel.

And in the previous video of API gateway, we have already seen how to perform a routing and how to

do the load balancing.

And today let's see how we can do authentication, circuit breaker, retry rate limiting etc. within

an API gateway.

So let's start without wasting any further time.

So to understand this we need to understand filters in API gateway.

Very important.

So filters can intercept and modify the HTTP request and response as they passes through the gateway.

Okay.

So there are two types of filter.

One is global filter.

Another is route specific filter.

Sometime uh engineers also call it gateway filters.

So what are global filters as the name says applied to every request that goes through gateway every.

Okay.

And very good example could be of like or performing authentication.

You want to do logging.

So those are some of the very good use cases for the global filters.

And route specific filters are like, as the name says, again applied to specific requests that matches

a particular route.

Okay.

We will not apply them globally to all the traffic, no, only particular traffic which matches a particular

route.

Okay.

And very good example would be you can say or you use case would be rate limiting circuit breaker resilience.

Retry that.

If you have multiple microservices you want different different configuration for for this circuit breaker

configuration could be different.

For this circuit breaker configuration could be different.

Okay I don't want to apply globally to all my to all the routes okay.

So we will see that.

But for now just have this definition like basic understanding and later on it will get more clarified.

So before we proceed further on the global filters and route specific filters, this flow we need to

understand properly.

Okay, this is the overall flow.

When a request comes to API gateway and its complete life cycle.

So now let's say the client hit the request localhost 8083 slash products slash one.

So this is our 8083 is our API gateway port number.

The request first goes to dispatcher handler dispatcher handler.

Okay.

After that it pass it to route mapping handler.

So what route mapping handler do.

Is it based on this route slash products.

It looks in our application.properties we define the routes right.

Route zero, route one.

So route zero is for product, route one is for orders and some certain configuration.

So based on the route let's say slash products, it define which config to use.

Like I show uh client slash products.

Right.

So it would use slash products slash asterisk asterisk.

So it would use this route route zero okay.

So it will create a route object.

After that there could be one.

There could be many global filters for each global filter.

A pre filter logic will run.

After that it invokes the next next filter in the chain.

So you can consider this as a chain of filters okay.

So first the pre filter logic You can say that logic runs.

After that.

It invokes the next filter.

So the next filter of global filter will get invoked.

If there are no more global filters, it will invoke the route specific filters.

Again, there can be many route specific filters, and for each filter there could be pre logic and

post logic.

So first it runs the pre logic.

And after running the pre logic it invokes the next filter in the chain.

So again the next filter could be route or next filter could be your again global filter also.

So now what happened in this there is a one global filter called route two request URL filter.

What it will do is whatever the path we are getting let's say slash products slash one.

We have to create a proper endpoint right.

Callable endpoint point because we are invoking it with the API gateway URL.

Write localhost 8083.

And based on this route we have to create a endpoint.

So currently our URI is load balancer.

So internally it has to call service discovery.

Then it do load balancing and then take out a one particular instance.

So that is all done by by this filter which is a global filter.

And then this ultimately pass to another global filter which is.

Net routing filter.

It task is to actually invoke the microservice.

And finally the microservice return the response.

And this.

Net routing filter which is a global filter.

It goes back to the.

Route specific filter okay.

And from there it will run.

It's the post filter logic.

So understand from this you have a pre logic.

Then you have like next and then post logic.

So this is the same code okay.

So pre logic run.

Then in the next it invokes another filter.

That task is completed.

Control goes back here.

And then it runs the post logic.

So the same thing okay.

So it control goes back.

And then it run the post filter logic.

So if you have multiple route specific filter.

So now all their post filter logic will run after that.

When all finish it the control goes back to the global filter this one.

And from there it will now run its post filter logic.

Okay, after that all this finished.

It runs one global filter at last, which is Netty writer response filter.

Its task is to forward the response to the client.

Okay.

So this is the overall flow.

So now here one question should have come to your mind is that hey, I see that global filters are before

the route specific filter also.

is given an order.

order value minus one will run before filter with order value zero.

--- Central Configuration ---

Hey guys.

Welcome back to the channel.

And today in spring microservices we are going to do centralized configuration.

And I can say that in most of the companies which are dealing with the spring boot, they are following

the centralized configuration approach only for managing the configurations.

But let's see the problem first.

And with that you will get to know better understanding that why?

Why everyone is following the centralized configuration approach okay.

The first problem is rebuild and redeploy.

Assume that this is your application order application and this is the Application.properties where

you are maintaining your configuration.

Now say that you have to update this configuration.

What you have to do.

You have to edit the application.properties file.

And after that you have to rebuild your jar and then redeploy the surface okay.

So just one configuration update, you have to rebuild and redeploy.

And with that itself, you can know that it's a big pain.

Okay.

Now the problem two is that inconsistent config across services.

Many times what happens is there have similar configuration across multiple microservices like this.

Let's say uh I have a very big order microservices which I have further divided into two smaller microservices.

One is pre order and another is post order application.

So both are different microservices but both helps in fulfilling the order itself pre order and post

order.

So definitely that the DB which they are pointing to with the same DB.

Okay.

And this is just a one configuration.

There can be many configuration which are common across multiple microservices.

So this is just one example.

So whenever you are making any update or adding a new property file, you have to make sure that it

is consistent across all the microservices.

Okay.

So that is another challenge.

Okay.

Now let's say that you change the DB.

You have made the change to order hyphen DB to something else, and you made only changes in the pre

order.

You forgot to make change in to the post order application.

Then it's become inconsistent configuration across different applications.

The third problem is no runtime update.

This is very important.

Now let's say that your application is already running in live and you need to change certain configuration.

Okay.

Then how your application which is running in live can see those configuration changes.

Is it possible?

Currently no because Application.properties file is loaded only once at startup.

When your application is getting, you are starting your application.

Let's say order application only that time it read application.properties file okay.

After that, if you make any changes into this one, there is no way that runtime itself while this

application is running it, observe the changes.

Okay, this has been updated.

There is no way.

Okay, so you have to stop it, okay?

You have to rebuild it and then you have to deploy it again.

Currently this is the way.

Okay.

If you have application.properties with the particular microservice, what is the problem for time consuming

rollback.

I told you that if any configuration change you make, you have to let's say raise the PR, get it merged.

Then you have to build the service again and then you have to redeploy.

Everything has to be done.

And if any issue comes, you have to follow the complete process again, which is a time consuming process.

Okay.

So that's what I have mentioned it here.

You have to manually add it.

You have to rebuild it.

You have to redeploy.

If multiple servers are impacted you need to fix all like in this example.

Let's say if db url has you have done misconfiguration, then you have to do the same process for this

pre-order application.

You have to the same process for post order application.

Okay.

So the rollback is time consuming.

So what's the solution for that solution for is centralized configuration with spring cloud config.

Very important you will find mostly companies are using this okay.

So here the first, you can say that this is the outer layer one.

In the first layer, which is we have our config files.

So we create a git repository just to store the config files.

Okay.

This is where we put our dot properties files.

Okay.

Even though you have multiple microservices for all the microservices we we have to put the configuration

files dot property files here in this git repository.

Okay.

So this is our first thing we will see.

We will go in depth of each of layer separately.

the property files.
